topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with oppressive monotony. Sarah stared at the stack of manuals on her desk, a tightness coiling in her chest. "So, you'll be taking over the accounts department," her manager, Mr. Henderson, announced, his tone suggesting this was a minor administrative task. Sarah felt a slow burn start in her jaw. She straightened her spine, the movement stiff and deliberate. "I suppose so," she managed, her voice unnaturally level. "I'll need access to the complete operational guide, and a full day dedicated solely to this transition." She didn't look up, focusing instead on the microscopic print of a procedure diagram, every line feeling like an insult to her years of accumulated expertise.

[story 2]

Mark felt a sudden, hot flush creep up his neck. When the VP of Operations laid out the training schedule, detailing every tedious step he would have to repeat, Mark’s knuckles turned white where he gripped his pen. He shifted in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning beneath him. "This is… a significant handover," he said, ensuring his voice carried a carefully modulated edge of disapproval. He crossed his arms tightly over his chest, a defensive posture he hadn't realized he was adopting. *They expect me to just hand over the keys like this? After everything I’ve built here?* The silence that followed his statement felt heavy, weighted by unspoken expectations.

[story 3]

"I’ll be your mentor," Brenda stated, clapping her hands together with an almost patronizing cheerfulness. As she began listing the onboarding requirements, a sharp, unpleasant sensation prickled behind David’s eyes. He leaned back in his chair, his posture becoming rigidly upright, almost confrontational. "Mentor," he repeated flatly, letting the word hang in the air like a challenge. He picked up a stray paperclip and began meticulously turning it over and over in his fingers, the repetitive motion a silent protest against the perceived condescension in her tone. He wasn't going to let this feel like a casual delegation.
